title: Daily Temperatures
description: Given an array of integers temperatures representing the daily temperatures, return an array answer such that answer[i] is the number of days you have to wait after the ith day to get a warmer temperature. If there is no future day for which this is possible, keep answer[i] == 0 instead.
thinking_questions: For each day you need the next day that's warmer — brute force scans right for each index. Can you avoid redundant scanning?
If you keep a list of 'waiting' days that haven't found a warmer day yet, when do you resolve them?
When you see a warmer day, which waiting days can you resolve?

hints.L1: For each temperature, you need the next higher one. Instead of scanning right for each, think about processing left-to-right and resolving waiting days when you find something warmer.
hints.L2: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. For each new temp, pop stack while top is cooler — answer for popped index = current_index - popped_index.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: monotonic decreasing stack — pop when warmer found
stack = []  # indices with no warmer day found yet
result = [0] * len(temperatures)
for i, temp in enumerate(temperatures):
    while stack and temperatures[stack[-1]] < temp:
        j = stack.pop()
        result[j] = i - j  # days waited
    stack.append(i)
return result
# Time: O(n) — each index pushed/popped once
# Space: O(n)

follow_up_questions: Why is the stack monotonically decreasing? What does that mean for the values in it?
Each element pushed/popped once — why does this give O(n) total?
How does this relate to the 'next greater element' pattern?
solution_notes: Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. Pop on warmer temperature, compute gap. O(n).
